topic: Does remote work improve or harm employee productivity?

sources.title: Does Working from Home Work? Evidence from a Chinese Experiment
sources.keyPosition: Remote work increases individual productivity by ~13% for structured, measurable tasks

sources.title: The Effects of Remote Work on Collaboration Among Information Workers
sources.keyPosition: Fully remote work narrows collaboration networks and reduces spontaneous cross-team communication

sources.title: How Well-Designed Hybrid Work Benefits Employees and Companies
sources.keyPosition: Hybrid arrangements (2-3 days remote) maximize both individual output and collaborative quality

themes.name: Task type moderates remote work benefit
themes.description: Individual, structured tasks benefit from remote work; collaborative, unstructured tasks are harmed by it

themes.name: Hybrid as reconciliation
themes.description: Hybrid arrangements preserve both individual productivity gains and collaboration quality

convergence.claim: Remote work is not universally good or bad — its effect depends on task type

divergence.dimension: Whether fully remote is net positive or negative

positions.position: Fully remote increases productivity in structured-task roles
Fully remote weakens collaborative networks in knowledge-worker roles

gaps.description: Long-run career development and promotion equity for remote workers vs. in-office workers
Effects on junior employees who rely more heavily on informal mentoring

synthesisStatement: Remote work increases individual productivity for structured tasks but reduces spontaneous collaboration in knowledge-work contexts. These findings are reconciled by hybrid arrangements (2-3 remote days) which preserve individual output gains while maintaining collaborative network density. The productivity debate is not 'remote vs. in-office' but 'which tasks belong in which setting' — a question every team must answer for its specific work profile.
